title: No curl variant truly isolates one biceps head
body: Long head (peak) gets relatively more emphasis with the arm behind the body, short head with the arm in front — but EMG evidence supports a shifted emphasis, not true isolation. Both heads stay active throughout every curl variant.

title: Hammer curls are the one real forearm-thickness gap in most programs
body: Neutral-grip curls are the only movement that meaningfully targets brachialis/brachioradialis over biceps proper — not interchangeable with any supinated-grip curl. Skip them and you're leaving arm/forearm thickness on the table.

title: Cutting the bottom of a preacher curl short wastes the whole point of the exercise
body: Long-muscle-length training at the stretched bottom position is a real, useful hypertrophy stimulus — cutting it short because it's uncomfortable throws away the exact thing that makes preacher curls valuable.

title: Behind-the-back cable curls have the longest range of motion of any curl variant
body: Arm-behind-the-body positioning stretches the long head further than any standard curl setup can reach — which is exactly why it's the top pick for peak/long-head development.

title: Full-depth squats aren't proven better for quads specifically — the real win is elsewhere
body: Full ROM is the safer default for lower-body training, but the clearest hypertrophy advantage over partial squats shows up in the adductors and glutes, not the quads themselves. Don't oversell it as a quad-specific finding.

title: Squats barely grow the rectus femoris
body: The rectus femoris is biarticular (crosses hip and knee) and needs a hip-extended, knee-flexed stretch to grow well — a position squats structurally can't provide. That's the real reason sissy-squat-pattern exercises exist in a well-built quad program.

title: "Leg extensions are bad for your knees" is a myth — it's angle-specific, not blanket-true
body: Leg extensions produce higher knee stress near lockout; leg press produces higher stress in deep flexion. Neither exercise is categorically worse — for knee pain, ease off the specific range that provokes symptoms instead of avoiding one exercise wholesale.

title: Toe angle doesn't isolate VMO on leg extensions
body: The inner and outer quad heads do get separate nerve branches, but EMG research shows no reliable way to selectively grow one over the other by rotating the foot. "Toes out for VMO" is a persistent myth, not a real coaching lever.

title: The single best rectus-femoris exercise isn't in most leg programs at all
body: The reverse nordic curl is the strongest research case of any quad exercise for rectus femoris growth — true hip extension combined with deep knee flexion reaches a stretch position squats and leg extensions structurally can't.

title: "RDLs train biceps femoris, leg curls train the medial hamstrings" is an oversimplification
body: The bilateral RDL shows no significant activation gap between biceps femoris and the medial hamstrings — both get worked similarly. Treat the RDL as a genuine hamstring-and-glute compound, not a biceps-femoris isolation move.

title: Seated leg curls beat prone leg curls for hamstring growth, and it's not close
body: Leg curls done at long muscle length (hip flexed, like a seated curl) produced roughly 2.2x greater biceps-femoris-long-head hypertrophy than the same movement at short muscle length (hip extended, like a prone curl).

title: The best-evidenced hamstring exercise for injury prevention isn't a mass-builder at all
body: Nordic hamstring curls are linked to roughly a 51% reduction in hamstring strain injury risk across a meta-analysis of 8,459 athletes — the strongest injury-resilience evidence of any exercise in the research, and most programs don't include it.

title: Hip thrusts crush squats on glute activation — but that doesn't mean they build more glute muscle
body: Hip thrusts produce dramatically higher acute glute EMG than back squats. But the best longitudinal MRI study found squat and hip thrust training produced similar glute growth over 9 weeks despite that huge EMG gap. Muscle activation isn't a reliable growth proxy.

title: Neither squats nor hip thrusts meaningfully grow the muscle behind a "hip shelf" look
body: Gluteus medius/minimus — responsible for the rounded upper/lateral hip shape, not glute size — barely responds to standard hip-hinge or thrust training. It needs dedicated hip-abduction work, a gap in most glute programs.

title: "Hip thrusts are hardest at lockout" oversimplifies the actual mechanics
body: Squats and hip thrusts do emphasize different parts of the hip-extension range, which supports programming both — but some analyses find peak hip-thrust joint moments actually occur nearer the bottom of the rep, not at lockout.

title: Straight-leg vs. bent-knee calf raises really do train different muscles
body: This is one of the few "which muscle does this train" claims in bodybuilding that's simply correct: a straight leg biases the biarticular gastrocnemius, a bent knee shifts emphasis to the knee-independent soleus underneath.

title: One study found a stretched-only calf raise beat full range of motion for growth
body: Training only the bottom, stretched portion of the calf-raise range produced more gastrocnemius hypertrophy than full ROM in one RCT — a rare case where a deliberate partial range outperformed the full rep.

title: Spot reduction is a myth — you can't train abs to lose belly fat there specifically
body: Fat loss is systemic and driven by overall caloric deficit. Ab training builds and strengthens the muscle underneath, but doesn't preferentially burn fat from that region. Visible abs are mostly a body-fat outcome, not a training-location outcome.

title: "Upper abs vs. lower abs" isn't fully true, but there's a real modest emphasis shift
body: The rectus abdominis is one continuous muscle, not separately trainable halves — but crunch-pattern movements do shift work toward the upper segment while leg-raise-pattern movements shift it lower. A real, if modest, effect based on where the resistance sits.

title: The first half of a leg raise is mostly a hip-flexor exercise, not an ab exercise
body: Rectus femoris (a hip flexor) dominates the first ~45° of hip flexion in a leg raise; the abs only take over more sharply after that. Cueing a posterior pelvic tilt matters more than just "lift the legs" for actually training the abs.

title: Grip training and direct wrist curls build different forearm muscles
body: Gripping exercises primarily load the finger flexors. If the goal is visible forearm size rather than grip-sport performance, direct wrist flexion/extension work is the more efficient path — gripping adds variety and grip strength, not extra size.

title: Wrist extensors are a real, trainable muscle group almost nobody trains directly
body: The extensors are a distinct, smaller muscle group not automatically trained by grip or flexor work — a genuine gap in most forearm programs that skip direct extensor work entirely.

title: Heavy compounds already build real forearm strength before you add a single curl
body: Squat/bench/deadlift-style training produces measurable forearm strength gains as a side effect. Dedicated forearm work stacks on top of that baseline — it isn't starting from zero.

title: Direct neck training measurably increases neck size and strength in 8 weeks
body: An isometric lateral-flexion training study found meaningful cross-sectional-area increases in the trapezius and sternocleidomastoid plus substantial strength gains in just 8 weeks — real published evidence, not just bodybuilding tradition.

title: Neck training doesn't reliably prevent concussions or whiplash, despite the claim
body: An 8-week neck resistance program increased strength and neck girth in athletes but did not improve head-neck stabilization during forced impact simulation testing. Train the neck for size and strength — don't oversell it as injury prevention.

title: Neck training can meaningfully help posture and pain, not just aesthetics
body: A 12-month study in adults with chronic neck pain found dedicated cervical extensor training outperformed simple stretching for reducing disability and pain — real relevance for anyone with a desk-bound, forward-head posture.
